banner: Week 20 · Prove It — The Pythagorean Theorem
essential_question: Why does a² + b² = c² actually work, and how can its converse tell you a triangle is right before you ever swing a protractor?
objective: Justify why a² + b² = c² using the area/square argument, apply the converse of the Pythagorean theorem to determine whether a triangle is right, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on the Pythagorean theorem.

content.prompt: Without looking, draw a right triangle, label its legs a and b and its hypotenuse c, and write the Pythagorean theorem from memory. Then explain out loud, in your own words, why the theorem only works on a RIGHT triangle.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 18A & 18B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 18A/18B. We check the tricky ones: keeping radical answers in simplest form instead of a rounded decimal, remembering the converse always compares against the LONGEST of the three sides, and the 'estimate first' step from the Systematic Review. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: a² + b² = c² is true for every right triangle. Argue it from the area/square picture: build a square of side (a+b) two ways — once as four copies of the right triangle around a tilted square of side c, once as the two smaller squares a² and b² plus the same four triangles. What does that force to be true?

Claim 2: the converse works: if a² + b² = c² for three lengths (c the longest), the triangle they form must be right. Argue why a triangle with those three side lengths couldn't possibly close up at any other angle at that vertex.

Claim 3: sides 6, 7, and 9 do NOT form a right triangle, even though they close into a real triangle. Argue it — what does the converse actually check, and how is that different from the triangle inequality?

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 19 — Rationalizing Denominators — where you clear radicals out of the bottom of a fraction, a skill that will make hypotenuse and leg answers even cleaner to work with.

success_criteria.text: I can identify the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle and state the Pythagorean theorem without help
I can find a missing side of a right triangle using a² + b² = c², keeping radical answers in simplest form
I can use the converse of the Pythagorean theorem to justify whether a triangle is right

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W20 The Pythagorean Theorem.md

questions.prompt: In a right triangle, the side opposite the right angle is called the…
questions.explain: The hypotenuse is the side across from the 90° angle, and it's always the longest of the three sides.

questions.prompt: The Pythagorean theorem states a² + b² = ___
questions.explain: The squares of the two legs add up to the square of the hypotenuse — not the hypotenuse itself, its square.

questions.prompt: A right triangle has legs measuring 8 and 15. Find the length of the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has a hypotenuse of 12 and one leg of 6. Find the exact length of the other leg, simplifying the radical.
The figure shows a right triangle with legs 6 and 8 units marked on the grid. Use a ruler to measure the hypotenuse directly on the page, then check: does your measurement match c = √(6² + 8²) = 10?
questions.explain: Rubric: accept any ruler measurement within about half a unit of 10 — this confirms a² + b² = c² is a real, checkable length (36 + 64 = 100 = 10²), not just algebra.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain how you can tell whether a triangle with three given side lengths is a right triangle WITHOUT measuring any angles.
questions.answer: Square the two shorter sides and add them; square the longest side. If the two results are equal, the triangle is right — that's the converse of the Pythagorean theorem.
questions.explain: Full credit names the converse: comparing (shorter)² + (shorter)² to (longest)² and checking for equality.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 18 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
